planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in late summer or early fall, before the blooms emerge; do not disturb established clumps as they bloom most prolifically when pot-bound and undisturbed. Plant in a spot where the bare stems will not look odd — flowers emerge without leaves.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; red spider lily is a classic of Southern American gardens, beloved for its startling habit of sending up bright crimson spider-like flowers on bare leafless stems in September and early October — just as the first cool rains return after the summer dry spell, hence the name hurricane lily. The curled, reflexed petals and long projecting stamens give the flowers a wildly exotic appearance unlike any other fall bulb. Strap-like dark green leaves emerge after blooming and persist through winter, providing ground cover. Bulbs naturalize prolifically into large clumps and were traditionally planted on the perimeter of rice fields in Japan as a rodent deterrent — all parts are toxic to mammals.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in summer or early fall with the neck at or just above the soil surface; the neck should not be buried. True Amaryllis belladonna goes completely summer-dormant before blooming — do not water during the dormant period.

directions: Plant in well-drained, even dry soil in full sun; belladonna lily is a South African native that produces clusters of 6–12 large, fragrant pink to deep rose flowers on bare, leafless stems in late summer and fall. Like red spider lily, the flowers appear before the leaves — hence the name 'naked ladies.' The strap-shaped leaves appear after blooming and persist through winter and spring before dying back completely in summer dormancy. This is a true bulb of the Mediterranean-climate garden: it thrives on summer drought and baking heat while dormant, then blooms with the first fall rains. Requires excellent drainage and does not tolerate wet summer conditions. Very long-lived and increases steadily into impressive clumps.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring, pressing seed into firm moist soil; or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant. Giant coneflower can also be planted as a container plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist soil in full sun; giant coneflower is the most dramatic member of the Rudbeckia genus, growing 5–8 feet tall and producing large, glaucous blue-green basal leaves that make a striking presence even before the flowers appear. The 3–4 inch yellow daisy flowers with prominent elongated dark brown cones are produced prolifically in summer on branching tall stems. The bold blue-green foliage is the plant's secret weapon — it is one of the few native perennials with truly blue-toned leaves, providing wonderful contrast with other summer perennials. Birds feed heavily on the seeds through fall and winter if left standing. A native of the south-central United States, it is especially well-adapted to Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination, or sow in early spring with cold stratification; Gulf Coast penstemon performs best in partial shade in the intense summer heat of zones 7–9 and appreciates afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Gulf Coast penstemon is a regionally native Texas and Louisiana species adapted to the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coastal Plain where most other penstemons struggle. It produces lavender-pink tubular flowers on erect spikes in spring that are excellent hummingbird and butterfly attractors. Unlike western penstemons that require perfect drainage and full sun, P. tenuis actually grows in moist, even temporarily wet soils and tolerates the heavy clay soils common in Gulf Coast gardens. It self-sows modestly and can establish small colonies. A critical native plant for Gulf Coast pollinator gardens that is still under-utilized.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring, or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; crimson scabious also germinates readily from fall-sown seed. Prefers alkaline to neutral, well-drained soil and is short-lived but self-sows freely.

directions: Sow seed in well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; crimson scabious is an outstanding pollinator plant producing dozens of rich crimson to burgundy pincushion flowers on wiry, branching stems from June through frost — one of the longest blooming periods of any perennial. The deep jewel-red color is unusual among summer perennials and combines beautifully with silver foliage plants and pale lavenders. Butterflies, bees, and hoverflies cover the flowers constantly; it is among the top five plants for beneficial insect attraction in European wildflower garden research. It is technically a short-lived perennial but self-sows abundantly enough to maintain a permanent colony. Deadhead spent blooms to extend the flowering season and prevent excessive self-seeding.
